title: Two cooks at the refugee kitchen
summary: Two cooks at a Berlin refugee kitchen disagree about what to serve. One wants familiar food, the other wants local food. Both are right about a different hunger.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: A small community kitchen in Neukölln, Berlin, serves lunch four days a week to a hundred and twenty newly-arrived asylum seekers. The lead cook is Layla, who came to Germany from Syria eight years ago. The new volunteer cook is Tomas, from a German family that has lived in this neighbourhood for three generations. They are planning the next week's menu. They have stopped at Wednesday.

body.speaker: Layla
body.text: On Wednesday I want to make mansaf. It takes a long time, but it is the dish most of the new families would have eaten on a Friday at home. They have been here eleven days, sixteen days, three weeks. They have eaten potatoes and bread. Mansaf is a kindness. They will eat too much, and then they will sleep, and then they will cry a little.

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: I understand the impulse. But I would suggest, instead, a German one-pot dish — perhaps a winter eintopf with sausage and pearl barley. That is the food they will see in this country for the next thirty years. If we keep serving them what they had at home, we are postponing the moment when they have to eat what is here. That moment will come anyway. They will have to shop in the local Penny supermarket on Tuesday.

body.speaker: Layla
body.text: You are not wrong about Tuesday. You are wrong about Wednesday. They will not eat eintopf better because we forced it on them in week two. They will eat eintopf when their neighbour next door offers them some, and they accept, because they have learned to accept. They cannot do that yet. They can barely speak. The mansaf is not about flavour. It is about being recognised by a plate.

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: And what about the second-generation children? They are watching us. They will already learn to be German at school. If at home and at the kitchen they only eat home food, they will end up between two worlds, not in either.

body.speaker: Layla
body.text: That is not what happens. The children will be fluent in German by spring. They will eat döner with their classmates on the corner and complain to their mothers that home food is 'too salty'. Within five years they will be the ones translating for their parents at the doctor. Their German is not in danger. What is in danger is their parents' sense that their food, their work, their lives existed before they arrived here in a coat that was the wrong weight.

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: All right. But you also know that some of these families come from places that hated each other. We have an Eritrean family at table four, an Iraqi family at table six. If we serve a strongly Syrian dish, the Iraqi family will be quiet. If we serve a strongly Eritrean dish, the Syrian family will be quiet. A German dish is neutral. It belongs to none of them. Maybe that is its honest function.

body.speaker: Layla
body.text: That is a fair point. I had not thought of it as neutrality. I had thought of it as imposition.

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: Both are possible. Same dish, different meaning depending on the kitchen.

body.speaker: Layla
body.text: Then let us try this. Wednesday we cook mansaf — and the Eritrean and Iraqi children come help. They make the bread. They taste. We talk about how their mothers cook it differently. Thursday we cook a Berlin eintopf, and a Syrian grandmother helps stir it. She tastes. We talk about what's missing for her. The dish on the plate matters less than who is standing at the pot.

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: I can live with that. I would also suggest one quiet rule. Whichever dish we serve, the recipe is on the wall in three languages. People can read while they wait. They learn the words for things they are eating. That is how my grandmother learned Polish, after the war, in a kitchen in Wedding.

body.speaker: Layla
body.text: Your grandmother was right. Recipe on the wall. Children at the bread. Grandmother at the eintopf. We are agreed.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: On Wednesday the kitchen smelled of yoghurt, lamb and saffron. The Iraqi family ate quietly but slowly, which Layla read as a good sign. On Thursday the eintopf had, by general agreement, too little salt; the Syrian grandmother said so loudly, the volunteers laughed, and three of the children learned the word 'salzig'. The kitchen is now writing all recipes on the wall, in Arabic, Tigrinya and German. It still serves a hundred and twenty people. The numbers, neither cook has admitted to the other, are slowly going up.

annotations.meaningVi: mansaf (món truyền thống Levant với cừu và sữa chua)
được công nhận qua một đĩa thức ăn
thế hệ thứ hai (con cái của người nhập cư)
döner (món thịt nướng Thổ Nhĩ Kỳ phổ biến ở Đức)

annotations.phrase: in a coat that was the wrong weight
annotations.meaningVi: trong cái áo khoác không phù hợp thời tiết
lúa mạch tròn (nguyên liệu nấu ăn)

grammarNotes.title: First conditional + 'if/when' for planning
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng câu điều kiện loại 1 và mệnh đề 'when' để lập kế hoạch hoặc dự đoán hệ quả. Trong bài: 'If we keep serving them what they had at home, we are postponing the moment when they have to eat what is here.' (1st cond — hệ quả thực tế). 'If we serve a strongly Syrian dish, the Iraqi family will be quiet.' (1st cond — dự đoán). 'Within five years they will be the ones translating for their parents at the doctor.' (will + V — dự đoán dài hạn). Câu 'when' cũng dùng cho thời điểm chắc xảy ra: 'when their neighbour next door offers them some'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses the 1st conditional and 'when'-clauses for planning and consequence prediction. In the text: 'If we keep serving them what they had at home, we are postponing the moment when they have to eat what is here.' (1st conditional — real consequence). 'If we serve a strongly Syrian dish, the Iraqi family will be quiet.' (1st conditional — prediction). 'Within five years they will be the ones translating for their parents at the doctor.' (will + V — long-term prediction). 'When' clauses also mark certain future moments: 'when their neighbour next door offers them some'.

translationVi:
Một bếp cộng đồng nhỏ ở Neukölln, Berlin, phục vụ trưa bốn ngày một tuần cho một trăm hai mươi người mới xin tị nạn. Đầu bếp chính là Layla, đến Đức từ Syria tám năm trước. Tình nguyện mới là Tomas, từ một gia đình Đức đã sống ở khu này ba thế hệ. Họ đang lên thực đơn tuần tới. Họ dừng ở thứ Tư.

Layla: Thứ Tư tôi muốn làm mansaf. Lâu, nhưng đó là món hầu hết các gia đình mới đến đã từng ăn tối thứ Sáu ở nhà. Họ ở đây mười một, mười sáu, hai mươi mốt ngày. Họ ăn khoai và bánh mì. Mansaf là một sự tử tế. Họ sẽ ăn quá nhiều, rồi ngủ, rồi khóc một chút.

Tomas: Tôi hiểu thôi thúc. Nhưng tôi đề nghị, thay vào đó, một món Đức một-nồi — eintopf mùa đông với xúc xích và mạch. Đó là món họ sẽ thấy ở đất nước này trong ba mươi năm tới. Nếu cứ phục vụ những gì họ ăn ở nhà, ta đang trì hoãn khoảnh khắc họ phải ăn cái ở đây. Khoảnh khắc đó sẽ đến dù sao. Họ sẽ phải đi siêu thị Penny thứ Ba.

Layla: Anh không sai về thứ Ba. Anh sai về thứ Tư. Họ sẽ không ăn eintopf ngon hơn vì ta ép trong tuần hai. Họ sẽ ăn eintopf khi hàng xóm bên cạnh mời, và họ nhận, vì đã học cách nhận. Họ chưa làm được. Họ gần như không nói được. Mansaf không phải về hương vị. Đó là về việc được công nhận bằng một đĩa.

Tomas: Còn các đứa trẻ thế hệ hai? Chúng đang nhìn ta. Chúng sẽ học làm người Đức ở trường. Nếu ở nhà và ở bếp chỉ ăn món quê, chúng sẽ kẹt giữa hai thế giới, không thuộc thế giới nào.

Layla: Không phải vậy. Bọn trẻ sẽ thạo tiếng Đức trước mùa xuân. Chúng sẽ ăn döner với bạn ngoài góc phố và than với mẹ rằng đồ ăn nhà 'mặn quá'. Trong năm năm chúng sẽ là người dịch cho cha mẹ ở phòng khám. Tiếng Đức của chúng không nguy. Cái nguy là cảm giác của cha mẹ chúng rằng món ăn, công việc, đời sống đã tồn tại trước khi họ đến đây trong chiếc áo khoác không hợp thời tiết.

Tomas: Được. Nhưng cũng phải biết một số gia đình đến từ những nơi từng ghét nhau. Có gia đình Eritrea bàn bốn, gia đình Iraq bàn sáu. Nếu phục vụ một món rặt Syria, gia đình Iraq sẽ im. Nếu phục vụ một món rặt Eritrea, gia đình Syria sẽ im. Món Đức thì trung lập. Không thuộc về ai. Có khi đó là chức năng trung thực của nó.

Layla: Đó là điểm công bằng. Tôi đã không nghĩ về nó như sự trung lập. Tôi đã nghĩ về nó như áp đặt.

Tomas: Cả hai đều có thể. Cùng món, nghĩa khác tuỳ bếp.

Layla: Vậy thử thế này. Thứ Tư nấu mansaf — trẻ em Eritrea và Iraq tới phụ. Chúng làm bánh. Chúng nếm. Ta nói về việc mẹ chúng nấu khác thế nào. Thứ Năm nấu eintopf Berlin, một bà Syria phụ khuấy. Bà nếm. Ta nói cái gì còn thiếu với bà. Món trên đĩa quan trọng ít hơn người đứng ở nồi.

Tomas: Tôi chịu được. Tôi cũng đề nghị một quy tắc lặng. Dù phục vụ món nào, công thức dán trên tường ba thứ tiếng. Người ta đọc khi chờ. Họ học chữ chỉ thứ họ đang ăn. Đó là cách bà tôi học tiếng Ba Lan, sau chiến tranh, trong một bếp ở Wedding.

Layla: Bà anh đúng. Công thức trên tường. Trẻ em ở bánh. Bà ở eintopf. Đồng ý.

Thứ Tư bếp thơm mùi sữa chua, cừu, nghệ tây. Gia đình Iraq ăn im nhưng chậm, Layla đọc là dấu hiệu tốt. Thứ Năm eintopf, theo công nhận chung, ít muối; bà người Syria nói to, tình nguyện viên cười, ba đứa trẻ học từ 'salzig'. Bếp giờ viết mọi công thức trên tường tiếng Ả Rập, Tigrinya, Đức. Vẫn phục vụ một trăm hai mươi người. Con số, mà không ai trong hai đầu bếp thừa nhận với nhau, đang tăng lên chậm.

questions.prompt: Where is the kitchen and who comes?
options: Neukölln, Berlin; 120 newly arrived asylum seekers, 4 days a week
Layla (lead cook, came from Syria 8 years ago) and Tomas (new volunteer, German family in the neighbourhood 3 generations)

questions.prompt: What does Layla want to cook on Wednesday and why?
options: Mansaf — most of the new families would have eaten it on Fridays at home; 'being recognised by a plate'

questions.prompt: What does Tomas suggest and why?
options: A winter eintopf with sausage and pearl barley — the food they will see in this country for the next 30 years

questions.prompt: What is Layla's worry about the children if they ONLY eat home food?
options: Layla actually argues that children's German is not in danger; what is in danger is their parents' sense of pre-arrival life

questions.prompt: What new argument does Tomas raise about table dynamics?
options: Eritrean family at table 4, Iraqi at table 6 — a strongly national dish silences other families; a German dish is 'neutral'

questions.prompt: How does Layla respond to the neutrality argument?
options: Fair point — she'd thought of it as imposition, not neutrality
Wednesday mansaf with Eritrean and Iraqi kids helping; Thursday eintopf with a Syrian grandmother stirring; 'who is standing at the pot matters more than the dish'
questions.hint: Layla's 'who is standing at the pot' line.

questions.prompt: What does Tomas add as a quiet rule?
options: Recipe on the wall in three languages — like his grandmother learning Polish after the war in Wedding

questions.prompt: What happened on Wednesday and Thursday?
options: Wednesday: yoghurt/lamb/saffron, Iraqi family ate slowly (good sign); Thursday: eintopf too little salt, Syrian grandmother said so loudly, kids learned 'salzig'

questions.prompt: What has the kitchen now done, and what trend has neither cook mentioned?
options: All recipes on the wall in Arabic, Tigrinya, German; numbers slowly going up
Familiar food and local food serve two different real hungers — and a well-run kitchen can serve both by paying attention to who is at the pot, not only to what is in it.
Refugees should not eat German food.

cloze.template:
Narrator: A small community kitchen in Neukölln, Berlin, serves lunch four days a week to a hundred and twenty newly-arrived asylum seekers. The lead cook is Layla, who came to Germany from Syria eight years ago. The new volunteer cook is Tomas, from a German family that has lived in this neighbourhood for three generations. They are planning the next week's menu. They have stopped at {{b1}}.

They have been here eleven days, sixteen days, three weeks. They have eaten potatoes and bread. Mansaf is a {{b2}}. They will eat too much, and then they will sleep, and then they will cry a little.
Tomas: I understand the impulse. But I would suggest, instead, a German one-pot dish — perhaps a winter eintopf with sausage and pearl {{b3}}. That is the food they will see in this country for the next thirty years. If we keep serving them what they had at home, we are postponing the moment when they have to eat what is here. That moment will come anyway. They will have to shop in the local Penny supermarket on Tuesday.
Layla: You are not wrong about Tuesday. You are wrong about Wednesday. They will not eat eintopf better because we forced it on them in week two. They will eat eintopf when their neighbour next door offers them some, and they accept, because they have learned to accept. They cannot do that yet. They can barely speak. The mansaf is not about flavour. It is about being recognised by a {{b4}}.
Tomas: And what about the second-generation children? They are watching us. They will already learn to be German at school. If at home and at the kitchen they only eat home food, they will end up between two worlds, not in either.
Layla: That is not what happens. The children will be fluent in German by spring. They will eat döner with their classmates on the corner and complain to their mothers that home food is 'too salty'. Within five years they will be the ones {{b5}} for their parents at the doctor. Their German is not in danger. What is in danger is their parents' sense that their food, their work, their lives existed before they arrived here in a coat that was the wrong {{b6}}.
Tomas: All right. But you also know that some of these families come from places that hated each other. We have an Eritrean family at table four, an Iraqi family at table six. If we serve a strongly Syrian dish, the Iraqi family will be quiet. If we serve a strongly Eritrean dish, the Syrian family will be quiet. A German dish is {{b7}}. It belongs to none of them. Maybe that is its honest function.
Layla: That is a fair point. I had not thought of it as neutrality. I had thought of it as {{b8}}.
Tomas: Both are possible. Same dish, different meaning depending on the kitchen.
Layla: Then let us try this. Wednesday we cook mansaf — and the Eritrean and Iraqi children come help. They make the bread. They taste. We talk about how their mothers cook it differently. Thursday we cook a Berlin eintopf, and a Syrian grandmother helps stir it. She tastes. We talk about what's missing for her. The dish on the plate matters less than who is standing at the {{b9}}.
Tomas: I can live with that. I would also suggest one quiet rule. Whichever dish we serve, the {{b10}} is on the wall in three languages. People can read while they wait. They learn the words for things they are eating. That is how my grandmother learned Polish, after the war, in a kitchen in Wedding.
Layla: Your grandmother was right. Recipe on the wall. Children at the bread. Grandmother at the eintopf. We are agreed.
Narrator: On Wednesday the kitchen smelled of yoghurt, lamb and saffron. The Iraqi family ate quietly but slowly, which Layla read as a good sign. On Thursday the eintopf had, by general agreement, too little salt; the Syrian grandmother said so loudly, the volunteers laughed, and three of the children learned the word 'salzig'. The kitchen is now writing all recipes on the wall, in Arabic, Tigrinya and German. It still serves a hundred and twenty people. The numbers, neither cook has admitted to the other, are slowly going {{b11}}.

blanks.explanation: Who is standing at the pot.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Layla says mansaf is 'about being recognised by a plate', and Tomas says eintopf is 'about what they will eat for thirty years'. Think of a meal someone outside your culture cooked for you — or that you cooked for them. What was each side trying to feed in the other person beyond hunger?
